story:
The front door creaks open as my hand reaches for it. I hesitate, then step into the dark Victorian house, following the flicker of a fireplace. He is there—Luis Vasquez, the most feared man in northern New Mexico—sitting beside a granite table, smoking, thinking. His voice is low, steady, and the door slams shut behind me. He asks if I’ve come to repay him. I tell him my father sent me. He studies me—eighteen, a student at Highlands—and mocks the school, boasts of a long, strange life, sunlight not his friend, a past with colonizers and priests, and a hand in founding institutions. He smells like smoke and winter.

I admit why I’ve come: I caused this. He rises, a tall shadow, and names my crime. I’m the one who killed his wolf. My hands shake as I pull out the police report. It was an accident, I say. It was late. The wolf leapt in front of my car. He says he should kill me. I remind him of his promise to my father. He says he can change his mind.

We stare. The paper slips from my hand. He circles the table and comes close, whispering that my life is over. I beg for a reason to be spared: I’m too young to die. I haven’t lived. He pauses, as if hearing something from long ago. He returns to the fire, takes up the iron poker, and gives me one question—answer wrong and he’ll drive it through my neck. What is the purpose of life? Not truth, not happiness, not family, not God, he warns. The front door opens. I bolt, slip, fall. He looms over me with the poker, demanding the answer I already know. Without thinking, I shout: to live. To live.

He drops the iron. Yes, he says. To live—and live long. He looks at me as if seeing his reflection. He says one day I might be like him. Then he remembers the debt. My father said I brought him payment.

I go out into the snow and open the trunk. Leo, my classmate, stares up at me—confused, taped, breathing fast. I hate what I’m doing. I try to pull him up the stairs, slip, hit my head. Luis comes and drags Leo inside like he weighs nothing. I crawl in after them, numb and shaking. In that cold room, Leo’s eyes plead. When Luis tears the tape away, Leo gasps and blurts, “Please, I know the answer of that question, the purpose of life is to live. To live.” He strips him of any chance and lays him on the granite. I shut my eyes as Leo prays. Luis tells him God won’t help. Outside, wolves howl. Luis bending to feed, Leo’s breath turning to a thin, vanishing sound.

I stagger out into the night. I get in my car and drive away, the road unspooling under my headlights, the answer burning in my chest like a curse: the purpose of life is to live. I am alive. And I will have to live with what that cost.
